strengths: Clear identification of client and authentic scenario
Rationale explains choice of SwiftUI and iOS platform
Comprehensive, specific and measurable success criteria linked to client needs
weaknesses: Evidence of consultation is limited to a single undated narrative
Rationale lacks comparison of alternative platforms and feasibility analysis
Some success criteria overlap; minor refinement needed for measurability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of three strands (scenario/client investigation and product rationale) demonstrate solid but partial work (band 3â4), while success criteria are comprehensive (band 5â6). Overall performance sits firmly in the middle of the 3â4 band, meriting 4 marks.

strengths: Implements AES encryption, role-based access, demand forecasting and cross-language integration, showing notable problem-solving
Effective use of Xcode, SwiftUI, external libraries and UML tools throughout development
Pseudocode and code snippets are generally clear and show structured algorithmic thinking
weaknesses: Source attribution is minimal (no version or license details) and not all assets are cited
Documentation lacks comprehensive explanation of error handling and performance considerations
Some pseudocode contains minor typos and missing exception branches
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates moderate to strong complexity and effective tool use (band 5â8) but lacks the comprehensive documentation required for the top band. A mark of 8 reflects solid mid-band performance across all three development strands.

strengths: Comprehensive design overview with UML diagrams, flowcharts and annotated mock-ups
Test plan links core functions to expected outcomes
Design documentation clearly shows overall architecture and control flow
weaknesses: No chronological record of tasks or Gantt chart to show process
Test plan lacks pass/fail criteria and detailed test data
Some diagrams (e.g. class diagram) are referenced but not fully presented
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design overview is thorough (band 5â6), test plan is basic (band 3â4) and there is no record of tasks (band 0). Synthesising these, the work sits in the 3â4 band overall, justifying 4 marks.

strengths: Screenshots indicate core features (dashboards, inventory and finance) function as intended
Modular design suggests the potential for future extension
Some UI interactions (e.g. form submissions) are demonstrably operational
weaknesses: No narrated video or systematic evidence to confirm all test cases pass
Role-based access is implied but not explicitly shown in screenshots
No discussion of handling error states or live data updates
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Core functionality is present but not fully evidenced (band 3â4) and extensibility is hinted at though not straightforward (band 1â2). Overall performance aligns with the lower half of the 3â4 band, warranting 3 marks.

strengths: Annotations include informal suggestions for future extensions (e.g., Android version)
Some reflective commentary in margins indicates awareness of areas for improvement
weaknesses: No formal evaluation against success criteria is presented
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there is no structured evaluation section, the few informal reflections and marginal notes show a minimal attempt at future planning (band 1â2). All other strands score zero, placing the work at the bottom of the 1â2 band and justifying 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple UML diagrams, flowcharts, annotated UI mock-ups and key algorithm descriptions together give a clear, structured picture of how all components interact. Control flow for login, dashboards, inventory and pricing is detailed, satisfying the descriptor of a "comprehensive design overview".

The rationale explains why an iOS application, written in SwiftUI, is preferable (client uses Apple devices, need for real-time access, waste reduction, financial tracking). Although these points justify the environment and features, the explanation remains general and does not weigh alternative products or discuss feasibility in depth, so it is best described as "partially explained".

The student employs Xcode, SwiftUI, an external encryption library (RNCryptor), UML design tools and image mock-ups effectively. Code snippets and explanations indicate appropriate and largely effective use of these tools throughout development, meriting a solid mid-band score.

A tabular test plan links six tests to core functions and records expected outcomes, but coverage of every success criterion (e.g., iOS compatibility, cost reimbursement formula) is incomplete and there is no specification of test data or pass/fail criteria. This aligns with a "basic outline" rather than the fully detailed top band.
Future improvements or recommendations are not discussed; consequently this strand receives zero.

The external library is referenced with a URL and its purpose is explained. Key algorithms are annotated. However, citation practice is minimal (no version, licence or full bibliography) and not all borrowed assets are acknowledged, so documentation is adequate but not "comprehensive".
No chronological record-of-tasks table, timeline or Gantt chart is included in the submission; therefore there is no evidence for this strand.

The app manages role-based access, inventory, demand forecasting, surplus calculation and AES encryption using an external library. These elements show more than a trivial level of algorithmic thinking and demonstrate moderate complexity and some ingenuity, but the solution does not reach enterprise-level sophistication required for the 9-12 band.

Ten success criteria cover interface design, role-based dashboards, iOS compatibility, finance tracking, waste reduction and data storage. They are specific, measurable and clearly linked to the clientâs needs; together they form a comprehensive set. Minor overlap exists between some points, so a top-band but not full-marks score is appropriate.
The dossier contains no written or recorded feedback from the client/adviser, so no credit can be awarded.

The separation into modular dashboards hints at future expandability, yet no explicit discussion or class-level abstraction is given. Modification appears possible but could be cumbersome, matching the middle descriptor where expansion is possible but not straightforward.

The student clearly identifies a real client (Mr Waghulde) and an authentic problem. A short set of interview questions and a narrative description show that at least one consultation has taken place, but the evidence is limited to a single paragraph and no dated notes or transcripts are supplied. Hence the work meets âevidence of consultation is statedâ but is not sufficiently detailed for the highest band.

Screen captures suggest core screens operate, but without a narrated video or systematic evidence it is unclear whether all features (real-time updates, forecasting) are fully functional. This points to partial functionality as per the mid band.
No reflective section compares the finished product with the stated success criteria; therefore this strand scores zero.

annotations.comment: The scenario description shows client needs but offers only a single paragraph of consultation. Add multiple dated interviews or meeting minutes to fully justify the clientâs requirements.
The rationale justifies SwiftUI for Apple device compatibility but does not compare alternative platforms or tools. Consider discussing options and feasibility analysis to fully justify your choice.
The success criteria are specific, measurable, and well linked to client needs, forming a comprehensive set for evaluating the solutionâs impact.
The pseudocode for Login lacks exception handling for corrupted credentials file or unexpected input. Add error branches to improve robustness.
Typographical error 'Fnd If' should read 'End If'. Ensure pseudocode uses consistent keywords to avoid confusion.
The interview questions lack context such as date, location and detailed client responses. Including dated transcripts or detailed notes would strengthen the evidence of consultation.
The product rationale remains high-level. To fully justify, include costâbenefit analysis or feasibility discussion (e.g., development time, maintenance) linked to client constraints.
Criterion âThe app should have an appealing designâ is vague. Define measurable design standards (e.g., WCAG color contrast, usability heuristics) to assess interface aesthetics.
The test cases cover core functions but omit pass/fail thresholds and specific test data. Define expected versus actual value ranges and sample datasets.
Encryption tests are missing. Include test cases for data encryption/decryption to ensure user data integrity and correct handling of HMAC and key errors.
There is no chronological log or Gantt chart for task tracking. A detailed record of tasks with dates would demonstrate a structured development process.
The application overview text is embedded as a header rather than a separable design overview. Consider isolating high-level architecture in a diagram section.
The Login flowchart figure is clear but does not show how encryption keys are retrieved. Annotate the decryption step in the diagram.
Consider adding a success criterion for security and encryption validation, as data protection is vital for user trust and regulatory compliance.
The test plan header lacks an overview of testing strategy, environment and criteria. Add sections for test data, pass/fail definitions, and responsibilities.
Inventory management pseudocode does not include a closing 'End For' for the loop over baked goods. Add loop closure for clarity.
Typo 'Flse' should be 'Else'. Accurate pseudocode syntax reduces misinterpretation during development.
The reorder algorithm suggests quantity but does not consider maximum stock limits or supplier constraints. Incorporate business rules for realistic suggestions.
Margin multipliers in pricing logic (1.5, 1.3, 1.1) are arbitrary. Define thresholds and justify the choice of multipliers with market analysis.
UI mock-up of the Login screen lacks annotations for accessibility (e.g., labels, contrast). Document how UI meets accessibility standards.
The Add Event UI form omits input validation (e.g., date format, attendee limit). Define rules and handle invalid inputs gracefully.
Typo 'Fnd For' should be 'End For'. Consistent loop termination improves pseudocode readability.
The UML Class Design section is titled but no actual class diagram is provided. Include a formal UML class diagram showing attributes and relationships.
The Sign-Up page form description lacks field validation rules (e.g., email format, password strength). Define and test these constraints.
Baker Dashboard screenshot does not specify confirmation for deletions. Include UI prompts or undo options in functionality design.
Common Dashboard sample uses static values; demonstrate real-time data updates and error states (e.g., network failure) for full functionality evidence.
Organiser Dashboard screenshot shows basic stats but omits explicit permission enforcement. Document how role-based access is implemented.
Management Dashboard UI shows finances but lacks notification for low supply or budget overrun. Consider adding alerts in the design.
Development description 'page by page' is generic. Link it to your task log or Gantt chart to show structured, iterative progress.
The loadData() function pseudocode is clear and well structured, showing appropriate error handling in decrypt operations.
Decryption catch block prints errors but does not notify the user. Add user-facing alerts or fallback logic to handle decryption failures.
Decryption process mapping is described well but lacks performance analysis for large data sets. Consider discussing complexity or optimization.
No section evaluates the finished product against each success criterion. Add a reflective evaluation showing whether and how each criterion was met or unmet.
There is no documented feedback from the client or adviser. Incorporate direct quotations or meeting notes to validate client satisfaction.
No future recommendations are provided. Propose realistic extensions (e.g., Android version, analytics dashboard) informed by evaluation and client feedback.
Encryption description confuses HMAC and master key roles. Clarify how each key is generated, stored, and rotated to avoid security misconfigurations.
The external library RNCryptor is referenced by URL only; include version, license and citation details to document proper source attribution.
The encrypted-data saving code snippet is presented as an image. Reproduce critical code as text with comments to improve readability and searchability.
Password-generation C algorithm description omits security assessment of randomness and seed. Include entropy discussion and validation steps.
Integration of C function into Swift is described but without code examples of the bridging header. Provide snippets to illustrate the interop.
